title: Regarding Waskom: Still Searching for a Sanctuary City
author: James Silberman
orientation: Free the States rebukes the 2019 Waskom, TX 'sanctuary city for the unborn' ordinance, arguing that exceptions and deference to the Supreme Court turned a righteous proposal into an iniquitous one that fails to treat abortion like murder. The piece contrasts Waskom's compromises with the no-exceptions hard line that produced Oklahoma's first abolition bill.
audiences: Pro-life Christians considering sanctuary city ordinances
Local legislators and city council members

questions.question: Why does Free the States refuse to call Waskom, TX a sanctuary city for the preborn?
questions.answer: Because the ordinance that actually passed contains exceptions allowing for abortion and explicitly conditions enforcement on Supreme Court permission, both of which surrender the moral and legal premise that abortion is murder. By those concessions, the city affirms rather than defies the legal regime that permits child sacrifice.
questions.quote: Waskom, TX is NOT a sanctuary city for preborn boys and girls. It is a city that almost passed a righteous ordinance but chose to pass an iniquitous and servile one instead.

questions.question: What was the original Waskom ordinance like before it was changed?
questions.answer: The original draft, brought by Jon Speed, Mark Dickson, and Rusty Thomas, contained no exceptions, no loopholes, and openly defied the Supreme Court — a genuine attempt at a sanctuary city ordinance.
questions.quote: The original ordinance they presented to the Waskom City council had no exceptions, no loopholes and legitimately defied the Supreme Court. Jon, Mark, Rusty, and others worked hard to pass a good ordinance.

questions.question: How does Silberman say the lawyer who reshaped the ordinance repeated the mistakes that produced Roe v. Wade?
questions.answer: Silberman argues that Justice Blackmun rejected Texas's 14th Amendment claim in Roe precisely because Texas itself did not treat preborn human beings as equal persons under its own laws — abortionists got fines while accomplices got nothing. By writing exceptions into the Waskom ordinance, the lawyer recreated that same self-defeating inconsistency.
questions.quote: Justice Blackmun, the author of the Roe decision, wrote that the State of Texas’ claim that preborn human beings had a Constitutional right to equal protection according to the 14th amendment was nonsense because even Texas didn’t provide preborn human beings equal protection.

questions.question: What is the theological case against writing exceptions into an anti-abortion ordinance?
questions.answer: Including exceptions abandons God's command 'you shall not murder,' fails the biblical duty to defend the fatherless (including children conceived in rape), and violates the command to love one's preborn neighbor as oneself. Compromise on these points surrenders the moral authority that comes from God's word.
questions.quote: by including exceptions and bowing to the Supreme Court, they’ve given up their moral authority. They’ve given up the Word of God which says “you shall not murder.” They’ve failed in God’s command to stand up for the fatherless, for who is more fatherless than a child conceived in rape?

questions.question: How does the Waskom ordinance compare to Greg Treat's Oklahoma SB195, and why does that matter to abolitionists?
questions.answer: Free the States booed Treat's SB195 out of the chamber for being a watered-down substitute for an actual abolition bill, and they consider Waskom's ordinance to be even worse. The comparison is meant to bind activists to a consistent standard: if SB195 deserved rebuke, so does Waskom.
questions.quote: When Greg Treat rejected an abolition bill and put forward a bill similar to Waskom’s ordinance, we booed him out of the chamber. The Waskom ordinance is actually worse than Treat’s SB195. It needs to be thoroughly rebuked by all abolitionists.

questions.question: What lesson does the 2016 Oklahoma abolition bill offer to legislators tempted to add exceptions to win passage?
questions.audience: Local legislators and city council members
questions.answer: Holding a hard line against exceptions in 2016 was uncomfortable, but it produced legislator and lobbyist converts (Senator Silk and John Michener), educated thousands of Oklahomans, and reframed the entire fight. Compromise would have produced a bill but none of the long-term movement gains.
questions.quote: Holding a hard line through that process made for some uncomfortable conversations, but is what educated the legislators, lobbyists, and public about abolitionism. Senator Silk, who authored the bill, is now a bold leader of the abolitionist cause. John Michener who lobbied for the bill is now a bold leader of the abolitionist cause.

questions.question: What does Silberman say is the proper response when a court rules that murder is legal?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christians considering sanctuary city ordinances
questions.answer: He frames it through a thought experiment: if the Supreme Court declared it legal to murder residents of Norman, OK or Waskom, TX, citizens would obviously ignore the ruling. The same defiance is morally required for the preborn, which is why conditioning enforcement on Supreme Court permission is unacceptable.
questions.quote: if the Supreme Court ruled that it were legal to murder people who live in Norman, OK or Waskom, TX, we would, of course, ignore that court decision. We need to do the same for our preborn neighbors.

related.title: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
related.reason: Provides the backstory on Greg Treat and SB195, which Silberman uses as the standard for rebuking the Waskom ordinance.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
related.reason: Develops the underlying principle that drives the Waskom critique: incremental, exception-laden bills cannot accomplish abolition.

related.title: Don't Fall For Texas Right to Life's Faux Abolitionism
related.reason: Continues the same theme of distinguishing genuine abolition from compromised pro-life legislation in Texas.

related.title: Tennessee SB 1236 Fails to Abide By its Own Preamble
related.reason: Another case study of a bill that talks like abolition but contains the same fatal compromises Silberman identifies in Waskom.

related.title: Supreme Court Idolatry at Texas Alliance for Life
related.reason: Directly addresses the 'bowing to the Supreme Court' problem that Silberman names as the central flaw of the Waskom ordinance.

related.title: No Exceptions
related.reason: Articulates the no-exceptions position that the original Waskom drafters held to and that the final ordinance abandoned.

related.title: Pro-Life Texas State Rep. Jeff Leach Delivers Death Sentence to 110,000 Preborn Human Beings
related.reason: Companion piece on Texas pro-life legislators undermining real abolition legislation, the same pattern Silberman sees repeating in Waskom.
